title: Swerve Drive Basics for FRC Robots
subtitle: A swerve drivetrain lets a robot translate and rotate by independently steering each wheel module.
summary: The 2024 robotics writing describes the team's move to swerve, PathPlanner, vision, odometry, and simulation.

artifact.title: Chassis motion becomes four module vectors
artifact.description: Change strafe and rotation to see why each wheel receives a different command.

sections.heading: What Makes Swerve Different
body: A tank drive changes direction mostly by varying left and right wheel speeds. A swerve drive can point each wheel module independently.
That means the robot can move sideways, diagonally, rotate in place, or combine translation and rotation.

sections.heading: Kinematics
body: The driver asks for chassis motion: x velocity, y velocity, and angular velocity. Swerve kinematics converts that chassis command into each module's speed and angle.
Odometry runs the other direction: it estimates the robot pose from gyro angle and module movement.

pitfalls: Trusting odometry after a gyro disconnect.
Forgetting to desaturate wheel speeds when commands exceed physical limits.
Testing only in simulation and skipping module zeroing on hardware.

quiz.question: What does swerve kinematics convert?
quiz.explanation: Swerve kinematics maps desired chassis velocity to wheel speeds and angles.
